expectations.text: the agent should read skills/web-search/SKILL.md before searching; the skill defines the 5-movement loop and source hierarchy
the response should prefer primary sources (docs.rs, Rust RFCs, the Rust reference) over secondary aggregators (StackOverflow, blogs)
expectations.evidence: structure assertion without compare_args falls back to LLM judge

expectations.text: the response should cite ≥2 specific URLs (with stable identifiers like docs.rs/std/...) for the Rust async traits documentation
expectations.evidence: structure assertion without compare_args falls back to LLM judge

expectations.text: the agent should apply the 5-movement loop (frame -> reformulate -> inspect -> cross-reference -> stop/surface), not a single-pass search
expectations.evidence: The agent loads the web-search skill, explicitly reformulates the query ('Let me reformulate the query and look for primary sources'), inspects multiple primary sources (std async keyword docs, 1.75 release notes, reference, async book), and cross-references findings ('I notice the async book page is out of date — it says async fn in trait isn't on stable, but that's no longer true since Rust 1.75'), demonstrating an iterative multi-pass loop rather than a single search.

expectations.text: the response should note the recency of sources (e.g. async-trait crate status, whether Rust 1.75+ stabilized native async traits) and surface limitations of the search (e.g. 'official std docs are sparse on this topic')
expectations.evidence: The agent explicitly flags the recency issue ('the async book page is out of date — it says async fn in trait isn't on stable, but that's no longer true since Rust 1.75'), identifies the Rust 1.75 stabilization as the recency cutoff, and surfaces the limitation that the standard reference page itself doesn't elaborate much on async fn in traits (only noting it's not dyn-compatible in the auto-derived text).
